coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from flax straw for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Guangkuo
Ge, Lin
Li, Yuanyuan
Mukhtar, Masood
Shen, Bing
Yang, Desheng
Li, Jiangong
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid by carbon dots are of importance for human health and environment. Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from biomass is essential for their sustainable applications. Herein, carbon dots were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using flax straw as carbon source. The obtained carbon dots possess a much higher quantum yield (20.7%) and excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. The “on-off” fluorescence principle, quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots in the presence of Co2+ or Cr6+ based on static quenching effect and inner filter effect, is extended to “on-off-on” principle for detection of ascorbic acid based on the reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ by ascorbic acid. The linear ranges for detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and ascorbic acid are 0–500, 0.5–80, and 0–200 μM, and the limits of the corresponding detections are 0.38, 0.19, and 0.35 μM, respectively. Compared with most reported fluorescence detections, our linear ranges are significantly wider and our detection limits are much lower for the detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and ascorbic acid. This sensing platform is highly sensitive and selective to monitor Co2+ and Cr6+ in real water and ascorbic acid in vitamin C tablets with remarkable practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Quantum yield
Fluorescence sensing
Fluorescence off/on turn
Detections of Co2+, Cr6+
Ascorbic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and AA
Fluorescence “on-off-on” turn sensing of Cr6+ and AA
Detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and AA in real samples using CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the CD synthesis from flax straw and the detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and AA.
TEM micrograph image (a) (the inset reveals the SAED pattern), size distribution histogram (b), XRD pattern (c), and FTIR spectrum (d) of the CDs.

Survey (a) and C1
S (b), N1
S (c), and O1
S (d) XPS spectra of the CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) (the insets A and B exhibits the optical photographs under sunlight and 365 nm UV lights, respectively), fluorescence emission spectra for the excitation wavelengths changing from 320 to 440 nm (b), normalized fluorescence emission spectra (c), dependence of normalized fluorescence intensity on pH (d), UV (λ ex = 365 nm) excitation time (e), and NaCl concentration (f) of the CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of the CD suspensions in the presence and absence of Co2+ ions (a) (the insets A and B are the optical photographs of the CD suspensions in the presence and absence of Co2+ ions, respectively), fluorescence spectra of the CD suspensions with Co2+ ions of gradually increasing Co2+ concentration (b), linear plot of fluorescence intensity change, (F 0-F)/F, as a function of Co2+ concentration (c), normalized fluorescence intensity of the CD suspensions with typical metal ions under the excitation of 360 nm (d), time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the CD suspensions with and without Co2+ ions (e), UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CD, CD/Co2+ (100 μM), CD/Co2+ (500 μM) systems, and Co2+ (500 μM) (f), fluorescence responses of the CD suspensions in the presence of Co2+ (500 μM) with 1500 μM Cys and GSH (g).

Normalized fluorescence intensity of the CD suspensions with representative anions under the excitation of 360 nm (a), fluorescence spectra of the CD suspensions with Cr6+ of different Cr6+ concentrations (b), UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CD, Cr6+, and CD/Cr6+ systems (c), time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the CD and CD/Cr6+ systems and the CD/Cr6+ system with AA (d), UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CD/Cr6+ (500 μM) system in the presence of different AA concentrations (e), plot of fluorescene intensity change, (F 0-F)/F, versus Cr6+ concentration (f), fluorescence spectra of the CD, CD/Cr6+ system, and the CD/Cr6+ system with AA (the insets A, B and C are the optical photographs of the CD, CD/Cr6+ system, and the CD/Cr6+ system with AA, respectively) (g) as well as the CD/Cr6+ system in the presence of different AA concentrations (h), plot of fluorescence intensity change, (F-F 0)/F 0, versus AA concentration (i), and fluorescence intensity change, F/F 0, of the CD/Cr6+ system containing different interferences under the excitation of 360 nm (j).
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for the detection of AA.
Detection of Co2+ in different water samples (n = 5).
Detection of Cr6+ in different water samples (n = 5).
Detection of AA in real sample (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from flax straw for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science and Engineering and MOE Key Laboratory for Special Functional Materials and Structure Design, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Science and Engineering and MOE Key Laboratory for Special Functional Materials and Structure Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Science and Engineering and MOE Key Laboratory for Special Functional Materials and Structure Design, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid by carbon dots are of importance for human health and environment. Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from biomass is essential for their sustainable applications. Herein, carbon dots were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using flax straw as carbon source. The obtained carbon dots possess a much higher quantum yield (20.7%) and excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. The “on-off” fluorescence principle, quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots in the presence of Co2+ or Cr6+ based on static quenching effect and inner filter effect, is extended to “on-off-on” principle for detection of ascorbic acid based on the reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ by ascorbic acid. The linear ranges for detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and ascorbic acid are 0–500, 0.5–80, and 0–200 μM, and the limits of the corresponding detections are 0.38, 0.19, and 0.35 μM, respectively. Compared with most reported fluorescence detections, our linear ranges are significantly wider and our detection limits are much lower for the detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and ascorbic acid. This sensing platform is highly sensitive and selective to monitor Co2+ and Cr6+ in real water and ascorbic acid in vitamin C tablets with remarkable practicality.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials and have drawn a lot of attention owing to their distinctive photoluminescence, excellent water dispersion, easy functionalization, good biocompatibility, and high photobleaching resistance [1–3]. CDs have shown promising application potential in chemical probes [4], biological imaging [5], and optoelectronic devices [6] due to their excellent optical properties. In recent years, different types of wastes and natural biomass such as waste polyethylene terephthalate [7], orange juice [8], sugarcane bagasse pulp [9], wheat straw [10], coffee grounds [11], egg [12], waste expanded polystyrene [13], and peach gum polysaccharide [14] (see Table S1 in Supplementary Material) were used as the green carbon source to prepare CDs because biomass itself can not only provide abundant carbon sources but also minimize the use of expensive and toxic chemicals. However, quantum yield (QY) of the obtained CDs is too low (mostly below 10%) and should be enhanced. The QY is a critical property of CDs and determines the down-to-earth applicability. Doping the CDs with a diversity of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus is an effective approach to enhance their QY. Among them, N doping is the most popular because N can change the electronic structure and create active sites in the CDs to broaden their potential applications [13,14]. A variety of methods such as arc discharge [15], microwave heating [16], electrochemical exfoliation [17], acidic oxidation [18], high-energy ball milling [19], and ultrasonic synthesis [20] were employed to prepare CDs. However, all these approaches involve more or fewer disadvantages including high cost, complicated purification procedures, expensive carbon precursors, harsh reaction conditions, and low yield. Therefore, facile, low cost, and green processes for preparing CDs are desired. Yuan et al. [10] synthesized CDs using the wheat straw as the carbon source through the hydrothermal method; their QY is about 9.2%. Liu et al. [21] reported the synthesis of CDs via the hydrothermal treatment of rice straw and ionic liquid. However, ionic liquids are expensive and not suitable for the large-scale production of CDs. Flax straw is waste left in the field after the flax seeds are harvested. 14,000 tons of flax straw was produced annually in the prairie of western Canada [22]. The problem with discarded flax straw for farmers is that the stalks are tough and difficult to be incorporated into the soil for recycling [23]. Flax straw contain cellulose, hemicellulose (~17%), and lignin and can be used to prepare CDs greenly. Thus, it is desired to utilize flax straw to synthesize CDs.

Cobalt ions (Co2+) are one of the important trace metal ions in the human body. A small amount of Co2+ ions can promote the production of red blood cells in the blood [24]. However, excessive content of Co2+ ions can cause Co poisoning and have negative effects on the human body such as emesis, paralysis, diarrhea, and hypotension [25,26]. Cr mainly exists in two oxidation states, trivalent and hexavalent, but the physical and chemical properties and toxicity of trivalent and hexavalent Cr are totally different. Cr3+ is found to be an indispensable trace metal ion in human body while Cr6+ compounds such as dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) and chromate (CrO4 2−) have higher mobility and carcinogenesis and is considered to be toxic to the human body [27]. Thus, the detections of Co2+ and Cr6+ are extremely important for the beforehand diagnosis of these diseases. Sundry methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry, chemiluminescence, and fluorescence spectroscopy were applied to detect Co2+ and Cr6+ [28–30]. But most methods suffer from high cost and tedious processing of samples before detection which limit their wider applications. Meanwhile, fluorescent probes are new types of detection methods having the advantages of facile operation and excellent sensitivity and are turning into promising analytical methods for detecting Co2+ and Cr6+. Ascorbic acid (AA) is one of the most important brain system neurochemicals that have antioxidant effects not only on cells but also on neuroprotection [31]. In addition, AA can support the formation of interstitial cells and maintain the normal function of teeth, bones, blood vessels, and muscles and enhance the immunity of human body [32]. Too low or too high level of AA is closely related to some diseases. Lack of AA in the human body may give rise to skin bruising, bleeding gums, muscle weakness, joint swelling, and edema whereas excessive AA intake can bring about urinary stones, diarrhea, and stomach convulsions [33]. Therefore, facile, straightforward, low-cost, and high-sensitivity probes for the detection of AA are essential for the early precaution of these diseases.

In our present work, highly fluorescent CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of flax straw. The non-fluorescent compounds form by the strong chelation or coordination interactions between the rich surface functional groups of the CDs and Co2+ or Cr6+. The non-fluorescent compounds further absorb the excitation light, resulting in an obvious fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, AA can recover the fluorescence quenched by Cr6+ through reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+. Thus an efficacious fluorescence “off-on” nanoprobe for AA can be achieved (Fig. 1 ). Furthermore, the “on-off” and “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors have distinct features of convenience, simplicity, time-saving, high sensitivity, and high selectivity.

Flax straw was obtained from local farmers (Lanzhou, China). The dialysis bags (MWCO = 3500 Da) and 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3-propanediol (Tris) were from Beijing Solarbio S&T Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). Fe(NO3)3, AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, Cd(NO3)2, CuSO4, Zn(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, FeCl2, MnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Na2CrO4, Na3PO4, NaClO4, Na2HPO4, glucose (Glc), citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), and AA were from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Glycine (Gly), glutamic acid (Glu), lysine (Lys), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), dopamine (DA), glutathione (GSH) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was utilized to prepare all solutions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (2θ scans) were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max-2400 X-ray diffractometer using Cu target and working with a current of 150 mA and a voltage of 40 kV. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were taken using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 field emission electron microscope working at a voltage of 300 kV. The size distribution histogram was obtained by counting 700 CDs in the TEM image. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by a Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrometer at 400–4000 cm−1. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Hitachi U-3900H spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra and lifetimes were acquired by an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrometer.

In a typical synthesis experiment, 2.4032 g flax straw was firstly carbonized at 250 °C for 2 h in air and the yield is 72.8%. Carbon flax of 0.24 g was then mixed with 50 mL deionized water and 500 μL EDA for N doping. The homogenous mixture was transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave after sonicated for 1 h and retained at 160 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was filtered and dried to obtain the product with a yield of 80%. In order to purify CDs, the product was re-dispersed in deionized water and put into a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) and dialyzed against fresh water for 24 h. The concentration of CDs in the resulting suspension after dialysis is 0.94 mg/ml.

QY was measured by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution as a reference [34]. For the purpose of minimizing self-absorption effect, the absorbance of the solution of the reference sample and the suspensions of CDs were controlled below 0.1 by dilution at the optimum excitation wavelength. The relative QY can be calculated through the following equation [35]
(1)
QY
X
=
QY
st
I x
A
st
A
x
I
st
η x
η
st
2
,
where I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A refers to the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The subscript “st” and “x” refer to quinine sulfate and CDs, respectively. For all these solutions, ηx/ηst is approximately equal to 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detections of Co2+ and Cr6+ were performed at room temperature as follows. In a typical procedure, 500 μL of the dialyzed CD suspension was diluted to 4 mL with deionized water and regarded as standard sample with a concentration of 117.5 μg/ml. Different volumes of Co2+ or Cr6+ solution were added into the standard sample and the fluorescence spectra were measured after reaction for 10 min. To check the selectivity of the CDs to the Co2+ and Cr6+ detections, different metal ions (Cd2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+) and anions (SO4 2−, Ac−, ClO3 −, ClO4 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−) with an ultimate concentration of 500 μM were added into the standard sample to examine their influence on the fluorescence of the CDs.

To detect AA, a certain amount of AA solution was added into the system of the CDs and Cr6+ with an ultimate concentration of 500 μM. To analyze the selectivity of the CD/Cr6+ system against AA, different interference agents including Glc, Tris, Gly, Glu, DOPAC, DA, CA, Cys, and GSH with an ultimate concentration of 1500 μM were added into the CD/Cr6+ system. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after reaction for 10 min at the optimum excitation wavelength of 360 nm with the emission wavelength range of 375–700 nm.

The tap water and river water was taken from our laboratory and the Yellow River (Lanzhou, China), respectively. The water samples were filtrated three times before analysis to remove large solids. Vitamin C tablets were pretreated according to the methods reported in literature [36]. Typically, five vitamin C tablets were ground into powder in an agate mortar, and then 0.15 g vitamin C powder was weighed, mixed with 30 mL deionized water, and sonicated for 20 min. The mixture was filtrated three times to remove the insoluble solids. Finally, the filtrate was diluted to 1% of primary concentration with deionized water.

The TEM image of the CDs synthesized from flax straw in Fig. 2 a shows that the CDs are nearly spherical in shape, fine in size, and fully dispersed. The size distribution histogram statistically determined from more than 700 CDs observed in the different areas of the sample by TEM in Fig. 2b indicates that the CDs have a narrow size distribution of 1.1–4.0 nm and an average diameter of 2.2 nm. The SAED pattern in the inset of Fig. 2a exhibits broad diffraction halos and a faint larger one, indicating the amorphous structure of the CDs. The XRD pattern of the CDs in Fig. 2c shows a diffuse peak centered at 2θ = 23.5° which again confirms the amorphous nature of the CDs.

The bands at 3396 and 2929 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 2d should be associated with the OH and CH bending vibrations [35,37]. The bands at 1639, 1580, and 1452 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN, CC, and CH, respectively. The bands at 1392 and 1073 cm−1 should result from the stretching vibrations of CO [37]. The XPS spectrum was measured to determine the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the CDs. The survey spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 3 a shows three peaks at 284.0, 399.0, and 532.0 eV which correspond to C1
s , N1
s , and O1
s of carbon (69.5 wt%), nitrogen (8.3 wt%), and oxygen (22.2 wt%), respectively [14]. The C1
s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3b can be fitted with four peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 286.1, and 288.1 eV which can be attributed to CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [14]. The N1
s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3c displays two nitrogen states of CN at 399.5 eV and NH at 401.2 eV [14,38]. The O1
s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3d exhibits two characteristic peaks at 531.2 and 532.5 eV which are associated with CO and COC/C-OH, respectively [14]. These results support the above FTIR analysis. The XPS and FTIR analyses indicate that the N doping has a significant influence on both the carbon core and surface functional groups.

The optical properties of the CDs were characterized by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 4 a reveals that the absorbance of the CDs gradually decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The CDs have strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, its absorption of visible light is mainly concentrated in the blue-green light region and extends to the near-infrared region, similar to the absorption spectrum of carbon nanoparticles and CDs prepared based on carbon nanoparticles reported in literature [39]. Not only do the CDs have broad absorption in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum but also have excellent fluorescence behavior, which is crucial for applications of the CDs. The insets of A and B in Fig. 4a show that the color of the CD suspension is pale yellowish brown under sunlight, bright blue-green light can be noticed when the CD suspension is placed under 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence behavior in Fig. 4b reveals an excitation-dependent emission behavior by increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 440 nm. Under the excitation of 360 nm, the optimum emission wavelength is 449 nm. The normalized fluorescence spectra in Fig. 4c shows that the fluorescent emission peak changes from 427 to 523 nm with respect to the excitation wavelength, which represents a down-conversion fluorescence behavior. These characteristics are universal in carbon-based fluorescent materials and probably originated from the diverse surface defect states and cores of the CDs of different particle sizes [13,14,40]. The QY of the CDs was measured to be 20.7% by using quinine sulfate as a reference. This QY value is significantly larger than that of the CDs without N doping (8.4%) and comparable to those of the N doped CDs reported [11,13,14]. N doping may produce more protonation process or aromatic structures, which is consistent with the XPS analysis result showing a large number of CN bonds in the carbon cores and amino. As a consequence, the electronic structure of the surface of the CDs is changed; and the promotion of electrons excited into the conduction band dramatically enhances QY [38].

The fluorescence stability of the CDs was examined at different pH values, different UV illumination time, and different NaCl concentrations. The effect of the pH value on the fluorescence stability of the CDs is depicted in Fig. 4d. The fluorescence of the CDs changes little in the 2–11 pH value range and decreases only below 2 or above 11 pH value, implying that the CDs can be applicable at both acidic and basic environments. Fig. 4e reveals that the fluorescence intensity decreases only 2.6% even after continuous UV illumination for 60 min, indicating that the CDs have good photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs in Fig. 4f is more or less immune to the change of the NaCl concentration even up to 3 M NaCl, indicating the high fluorescence stability of the CDs under high ionic strength conditions. All these results indicate a remarkable optical stability of the CDs, which has profound implications for sensing applications in vivo.

Due to their good water dispersion, excellent photoluminescence performance, and abundant surface functional groups, the CDs have potential applications in chemical sensing. We checked the influence of metal ions on the fluorescence of the CDs because the addition of metal ions to the CD suspensions can form complexes or chelates through strong interactions and trigger fluorescence quenching [41,42]. Fig. 5 a reveals that the fluorescence emission intensity decreases obviously when Co2+ ions are present in the CD suspension. The insets in Fig. 5a are the photographs of the CD suspensions with and without Co2+ ions under 365 nm UV light. We can see that the fluorescence of the CD suspension can be quenched dramatically in the presence of Co2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of the CD suspension in the presence of Co2+ ions decreases with increasing Co2+ concentration from 0 to 500 μM under the 360 nm excitation (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, a linear correlation between the change of the fluorescence intensity, (F 0 − F)/F, and the Co2+ concentration over the range from 0 to 500 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R 2) of 0.998 can be obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.38 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 11), and S refers to the slope of the linear curve). Kong et al. [41] reported the Co2+ detection using CDs with a linear range of 0–40 μM and a LOD of 0.45 μM. Our sensing platform presents a wider linear range and lower LOD. To check the selectivity of our sensing system, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was recorded with 500 μM typical metal ions including Co2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, and Al3+ ions (Fig. 5d). The introduction of Co2+ brings about a significant quenching impact onto the fluorescence intensity compared to other metal ions, demonstrating the remarkable selectivity of the CD sensing platform toward Co2+. In comparison to other fluorescence detection approaches for Co2+ reported (Table S2 in Supplementary Material), our method shows higher sensitivity and selectivity than most fluorescence methods reported and has the advantage of simplicity.

Two fundamental types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms, static and dynamic quenching mechanism, were proposed to explain fluorescence quenching. The static quenching mechanism is the formation of non-fluorescence complexes or chelation between the surface functional groups of CDs and the metal ions, causing fluorescence quenching. The dynamic quenching mechanism is the collision between metal ions and the excited molecules of CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching in the form of energy transfer or charge transfer. To further explore whether the mechanism of this “on-off” fluorescence probe is static or dynamic quenching, we perform the corresponding experiments. The Stern-Volmer equation can be utilized as a basis for discrimination of quenching phenomenon of the CD fluorescence caused by Co2+,
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
Q
,
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CD suspensions without and with Co2+ ions at the optimum excitation wavelength of 360 nm, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of Co2+ ions. The plot of the fluorescence intensity change, (F 0 − F)/F, versus the Co2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 500 μM in Fig. 5c shows a good linear correlation. The excellent linear correlation indicates that the fluorescence quenching is either due to a 100% static or 100% dynamic quenching effect rather than the static-dynamic combination [13]. The slope of the plot is Ksv = 0.0064 μM−1. For dynamic quenching effect, Ksv is proportional to fluorescence lifetime,
(3)
K
sv
=
K q
ι 0
,
where Kq is bimolecular quenching constant and ι 0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of the CD suspension. The Kq value was calculated to be 1.09 × 1012 M−1 s−1 using ι 0 and Ksv values. This Kq value is much larger than the maximal collision quenching constant reported (2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1) and in accordance with the static quenching mechanism. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime was measured to distinguish between dynamic and static quenching. As depicted in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs almost does not change on adding Co2+, indicating the ground state complexes or chelate formation may be the reason for the fluorescence quenching. This is in agreement with the result of the Stern-Volmer equation [43]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum analysis also supports the static quenching mechanism for the fluorescence quenching deduced from the Stern-Volmer equation and fluorescence lifetime. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CD, CD/Co2+ (100 μM), and CD/Co2+ (500 μM) systems and Co2+ (500 μM) in Fig. 5f confirms the above explanation. Compared with the CD and CD/Co2+ (100 μM) systems, the CD/Co2+ (500 μM) system shows a broad absorbance peak around 360 nm, implying that Co2+ changes the electronic structure of the CDs by forming a stable non-fluorescence ground state complexes or chelates between surface functional groups and Co2+ [13,44]. Moreover, the absorption spectrum of the CD/Co2+ (500 μM) system overlaps the excitation spectrum of the CDs. Thus, an inner filter effect (IFE) occurs. Naturally, the fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched more effectively because CDs/Co2+ can further absorb the excitation light [45]. Therefore, compared with low Co2+ concentration, Co2+ in high concentration can not only combine with the surface functional groups of the CDs to form the non-fluorescence ground state complexes or chelates but also yield the IFE effect and further quench the fluorescence. On the other hand, biothiols such as GSH and Cys can recover the fluorescence of the CD/Co2+ system [41]. The fluorescence emission spectrum in Fig. 5g reveals that both GSH and Cys can recover the quenching fluorescence. The enhanced fluorescence may be due to the stronger affinity of SH of biothiols than the surface functional groups of the CDs to Co2+ ions. Biothiols grab Co2+ from CDs/Co2+ to form Co2+/-SH. The enhancement of the quenched fluorescence and the above UV–Vis absorption spectrum further confirm that the quenching mechanisms for the Co2+ ions induced fluorescence quenching are the IFE and static quenching effect rather than the dynamic quenching effect [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “on-off-on” turn sensing of Cr6+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the Co2+ fluorescence quenching, Cr6+ can also quench the fluorescence of the CD suspensions. To check the selectivity of the CD suspensions for the Cr6+ detection, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs with 500 μM metal ions (Mn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, and Al3+ ions) and anions (SO4 2−, Ac−, ClO3 −, ClO4 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, and PO4 3−) was measured (Figs. 5d and 6 a). The introduction of Cr6+ into the CD suspensions causes a strong quenching impact to the fluorescence while other metal ions except Co2+ and all anions have negligible or no effect on the fluorescence of the CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the CD suspension decreases in the presence of Cr6+ with increasing Cr6+ concentration from 0 to 500 μM under the optimum excitation of 360 nm (Fig. 6b). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cr6+ in Fig. 6c shows two adsorption bands at 260 and 370 nm. The CDs exhibit an excitation peak at 360 nm and an emission peak at 449 nm. Obviously, the excitation and emission spectrum of the CDs overlap the absorption spectrum of Cr6+. Thus, Cr6+ yields the IFE effect between CDs and Cr6+ and subsequently turns the fluorescence of the CDs from “on” to “off” [45,47,48]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CD/Cr6+ system also shows two adsorption bands at 260 and 370 nm, implying the formation of the CD/Cr6+ complexes. The fluorescence quenching of the CDs by Cr6+ is static quenching, not dynamic quenching [45,49]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime was measured to clarify the mechanisms of the “on-off” sensing process. As shown in Fig. 6d, all the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs, the CDs in the presence of Cr6+, and the CD/Cr6+ system in the presence of AA are almost same, implying that the fluorescence quenching of the CDs induced by Cr6+ should be the static quenching effect [42,47]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CD/Cr6+ system with different concentrations of AA in Fig. 6e shows that the adsorption at 370 nm gradually decreases with increasing AA concentration. This implies that Cr6+ may be reduced to Cr3+ by AA through a redox reaction, which weakens the IFE and reduces the excitation light absorbed by Cr6+ and recovers the CD fluorescence. The fluorescence of the CDs is quenched by Cr6+ based on the IFE effect. Upon addition of AA, the “off” fluorescence of the CD/Cr6+ system is turned “on”. This represents an “on-off-on” strategy for the detections of Cr6+ and AA. Fig. 6f shows a linear correlation between the change of fluorescence intensity, (F 0-F)/F, and the Cr6+ concentration over the range from 0.5 to 80 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R 2) of 0.994. The LOD was calculated to be 0.19 μM. Compared with other fluorescence methods reported (Table S3 in Supplementary Material), our sensing probe based on the “on-off” fluorescence principle for the Cr6+ detection exhibits higher or comparable sensitivity and selectivity. Fig. 6g reveals that the presence of Cr6+ in the CD suspension decreases obviously the fluorescence emission intensity and the presence of AA in the CD/Cr6+ system recovers the quenched fluorescence. The highest fluorescence recovery ratio is up to 90.7% upon the addition of AA at a concentration of 1500 μM. The photographs of the CD suspension in the presence, absence of Cr6+, and the CD/Cr6+ system in the presence of AA under 365 nm UV light are in the insets of Fig. 6g. We can see that the fluorescence of the CD suspension can be quenched by Cr6+, and the quenched fluorescence of the CD/Cr6+ system can be effectively recovered by AA. Fig. 6h plots the fluorescence emission spectra of the CD/Cr6+ system with different AA concentrations from 0 to 1500 μM under the 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity of the CD/Cr6+ system increases gradually with increasing AA concentration. Fig. 6i displays a linear correlation between the change of fluorescence intensity, (F-F 0)/F 0, and the AA concentration over the range from 0 to 200 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R 2) of 0.998. The LOD was determined to be 0.35 μM. Tian et al. [50] reported silver-carbon dot nanohybrid for sensing of AA with the linear range of 0.2–60 μM and a LOD of 25 μM. Our linear range for the AA detection is much larger; and our LOD is significantly lower. The selectivity of the CD/Cr6+ system for AA was examined by evaluating the fluorescence intensity of the CD/Cr6+ system with 1500 μM representative interferences including Glc, Tris, Gly, Glu, DOPAC, DA, CA, Cys, and GSH (Fig. 6j). The fluorescence change ratio, F/F 0, is up to 9.1 upon addition of AA while the F/F 0 values for additions of other interferences are all below 3. Therefore, compared with the larger fluorescence induced by AA, the influence of other interferences can be neglected, confirming the excellent selectivity of the CD/Cr6+ system based fluorescence strategy for AA detection. This sensing platform based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle is highly sensitive and selective for the detection of AA and significantly better than most fluorescence methods reported for the AA detection (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Co2+, Cr6+, and AA in real samples using CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To check the practicality of our detection strategies, different concentrations of Co2+ or Cr6+ were added into real field water samples using the standard addition method and detected using our CDs. As listed in Tables 2 and 3
, for the detections of Co2+ and Cr6+ based on the “on-off” fluorescence principle in the tap water and Yellow River water, the fluorescence responses of our CDs to the Co2+ concentrations of 50, 100, and 200 μM are 49.7, 94.8, and 195.1 μM in the tap water and 48.7, 93.6, and 194.9 μM in the Yellow River water, respectively. The fluorescence responses of our CDs to the Cr6+ concentrations of 20, 40, and 80 μM are 19.6, 41.4, and 85.8 μM in the tap water and 18.7, 37.4, and 77.1 μM in the Yellow River water, respectively. These results show that the relative standard deviations of Co2+ are in the range of 0.6–6.4% with the spiked recoveries in the range of 93.6–97.5%. The relative standard deviations for Cr6+ are in the range of 0.2–2.0% with the spiked recoveries in the 93.4–107.2%. They represent a remarkable practicality for the detection of Co2+ and Cr6+ in real water samples. Similar investigations were performed with the CD/Cr6+ system in vitamin C tablets. Table 4 demonstrates that the fluorescence responses of the CDs/Cr6+ system to the AA concentrations of 0, 50, 100, and 200 μM are 38.1, 87.6, 145.2, and 224.9 μM, respectively and the relative standard deviations of AA are in the range of 1.0–2.6% with the spiked recoveries in the range of 93.4–107.1%. This sensing probe based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle to detect AA in vitamin C tablets is highly consistent with our expectations in the detection of practical samples, further confirming the reliability and feasibility of this sensing platform.

We developed a facile, green, and environment-friendly route for the synthesis of CDs from flax straw. The as-prepared CDs with abundant surface functional groups exhibit down-conversion fluorescence and the optimum emission wavelength of 449 nm. The QY under the optimum excitation of 360 nm is 20.7%. The fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by Co2+ or Cr6+. The observed fluorescence quenching may be due to static quenching and IFE effect induced fluorescence quenching mechanism. Meanwhile, the addition of AA into the CD/Cr6+ system may weaken the IFE effect and can recover the quenched fluorescence of the CDs. The ‘‘on-off” detections of Co2+ and Cr6+ exhibit wide linear ranges of 0–500, 0.5–80 μM with small LODs of 0.38, 0.19 μM, respectively. The ‘‘on-off-on” detection of AA presents a linearity in a wider range of 0–200 μM with a LOD as low as 0.35 μM. Moreover, this detection method is highly sensitive and can be applied in real samples for Co2+, Cr6+, and AA monitoring with good recovery. Due to the advantages including high aqueous stability, simplicity, high selectivity, and high sensitivity, the CDs are promising candidates for requests in chemical sensors and biomedicine and promote the application of carbon-based fluorescent materials in environment and biomedicine probes.

Guangkuo Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Lin Ge: Software, Validation. Yuanyuan Li: Resources. Masood Mukhtar: Writing - review & editing. Bing Shen: Resources. Desheng Yang: Resources. Jiangong Li: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51551201 and 51772137) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2019-sp03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.06.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
